locus_id: PAX3 (chr2q36; single WS1 locus, dedup of ~24 distinct PAX3 alleles across 24 mutation-bearing families + 7 original Waardenburg pedigrees)
coord_build: not_reported (1997 paper; legacy PAX3 cDNA/codon numbering, no reference build, no genomic coordinates)
pvalue: not_reported (abstract reports likelihood comparison of deafness penetrance across families; no p-value given; CSV holds per-family deaf/hearing heterozygote counts, not association p-values)
evidence_quote: Waardenburg syndrome (WS) is caused by autosomal dominant mutations, and is characterised by pigmentary anomalies and various defects of neural crest derived tissues. ... While mutations in the gene PAX3 seem to be responsible for most, if not all, WS type 1
classification_notes: PAX3 canonical (Waardenburg syndrome type 1); transcription-factor/neural-crest role established as background. This paper's own novel contribution is about deafness-phenotype penetrance (modifier-gene hypothesis), not the pigmentary mechanism -- but the attributed gene itself is unambiguously canonical.
author_explanation_quote: PAX3 encodes a transcription factor containing two DNA binding motifs: a paired domain and a paired type homeodomain...It is expressed in developing neural crest cells and in the brain
notes: IMPORTANT: contrary to the folder's ABSTRACT-ONLY.md marker (which the task described as indicating this paper is abstract-only/paywalled), the downloaded main PDF in this repo actually contains the FULL 6-page article (Introduction, Tables 1-4, Discussion, References) -- extraction was performed from the complete text, not just the abstract. This paper's own primary focus is on penetrance of DEAFNESS as a WS1 clinical feature (modifier-gene hypothesis), not on the molecular mechanism of the pigmentary phenotype per se; the PAX3-transcription-factor/neural-crest-expression mechanism for the syndrome (including its pigmentary component) is stated as established background, not this paper's novel finding.
